title: System Model: Input–Output Description
summary: This page describes translational mechanical system elements, focusing on the linear dashpot where force is proportional to velocity via the damping coefficient B. Example 1.18 presents a translational mechanical system with a mass, spring, and dashpot, asking for the input-output relationship between applied force x(t) and mass position y(t).

figures.caption: Figure 1.36 Some elements in translational mechanical systems.
figures.description: The figure shows three basic translational mechanical system elements. Panel (a) is a mass M with input force x(t) and displacement output y(t), panel (b) is a spring with stiffness K, and panel (c) is a linear dashpot with damping coefficient B. Students should connect these elements to their terminal relationships: mass relates force to acceleration, spring relates force to displacement, and dashpot relates force to velocity.

figures.caption: Mechanical system consisting of a mass M, spring K, and damper B driven by an applied force x(t) with output position y(t), shown in two physical configurations (a) and (b) and a free-body diagram (c).
figures.description: The figure shows a translational mass-spring-damper system with no coordinate axes, where the input force is labeled x(t) acting on mass M and the output displacement is labeled y(t). In configurations (a) and (b), the mass M is connected to a wall by a spring K and damping B, shown as either a separate dashpot on a frictionless surface or equivalent viscous damping at the base. The free-body diagram in (c) shows the applied force x(t) to the right and the opposing spring and damping forces Ky(t) and Bẏ(t) to the left. Students should notice that the two physical layouts are equivalent representations of the same second-order mechanical model.
